coredata.dc:description: The trace amount of water in organic solvents can affect the progress of chemical reactions, which will adversely affect chemical production in many industries, resulting in a doubling of costs. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) with abundant polar groups were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CDs showed superior dispersibility and fluorescence performance compared to the CDs that have been reported for the detection of water content in organic solvents. It can realize the fluorescence detection of trace water in several water-soluble organic solvents such as N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol and methanol with wide linear range (0 %–100 %) and high sensitivity. This will provide a powerful tool for the rapid detection of water content in organic solvents in chemical production.

Organic solvents
Water detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of water content in organic solvents
Water response mechanism of luminescence of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image (inset shows lattice fringe) and (b) Raman spectra of CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the prepared CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution elemental scans of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s of CDs.
(a) Emission spectra of CDs in different organic solvents and water; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in different organic solvents and water.

(a) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in DMF with different water content. (b) Linear relationship of emission peak wavelength with different water content in DMF. (c) Fluorescence intensity with water content in DMF. (d) Linear relationship of fluorescence intensity with different water content in DMF. (e) Color change diagram of CDs in DMF with different water content under UV light irradiation.
Preparation of CDs and determination of water content in organic solvents.
Comparison analytical performances of the CDs with other fluorescent sensors for detection of water.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for efficient detection of water content in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE
Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The trace amount of water in organic solvents can affect the progress of chemical reactions, which will adversely affect chemical production in many industries, resulting in a doubling of costs. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) with abundant polar groups were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CDs showed superior dispersibility and fluorescence performance compared to the CDs that have been reported for the detection of water content in organic solvents. It can realize the fluorescence detection of trace water in several water-soluble organic solvents such as N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol and methanol with wide linear range (0 %–100 %) and high sensitivity. This will provide a powerful tool for the rapid detection of water content in organic solvents in chemical production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, a large number of carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized and applied in various fields, especially in bioimaging and photothermal therapy due to their good biocompatibility and low toxicity [1,2]. Moreover, owing to the rich functional groups on their surface, they can combine with other ions and molecules to cause the change of their own fluorescence, so CDs are also considered to be the “right assistant” to realize fluorescence sensing [3,4]. In addition, electrocatalysis [5], electrochemical sensing [6], drug delivery [7], antibacterial [8] and other aspects also are important application fields of CDs.

In chemical reactions, organic solvents can interact with reactants, adjust reaction conditions, accelerate reaction rate or change reaction path [9]. In particular, in organic synthesis, organic solvents are often used as reaction media to provide appropriate environment and condition to promote the reaction [10]. The catalysis of organic solvents can make the reaction more efficient and selective, and improve the efficiency of chemical synthesis. However, some organic reactions inevitably generate water during the process, which will affect the normal progress of the reaction. For example, in most reactions involving organometallic compounds, the presence of water not only reduce the conversion rate and deactivate the catalyst, but also lead to different reaction products [11]. Therefore, chemical workers, including some chemical experts, always consider that water is a “troublemaker” in organic synthesis reactions. In addition, organic solvents have certain toxicity in industrial production and might cause certain pollution to the environment. There are also some literature reports that other substitutes can replace organic solvents, but they will lead to longer time and cost in industrial production [12,13]. On the other hand, in many large industrial productions, it is also likely that the presence of a small amount of water in the organic solvent leads to production failure. Therefore, it is necessary to realize the rapid and accurate detection of water in organic solvents. At present, many techniques have been proposed for the detection of water in organic solvents, among which fluorescence spectroscopy is more widely popular. In particular, so far various materials, such as covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [14], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [15], CDs [16] and organic small molecule probes [17], have been exploited as fluorescence sensing materials for the sensitive detection of water in organic solvents. However, limited by poor dispersibility, they are not considered to be the best choice.

Based on above consideration, in this work, highly disperse water-soluble CDs were synthesized from o-phenylenediamine by catalytic oxidation of FeCl3. The obtained CDs can be directly used to determine the water content in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EA) and methanol (MT), and the linear range is expanded to 0.01 %–99.9 % (Scheme 1 ). Especially, the fluorescent color change of the prepared carbon dot test strip under UV lamp can realize the rapid detection of water content in organic solvents. This will be of great practical significance to chemical industry production and rapid detection of water content in organic solvents under laboratory conditions.
Materials and apparatus used can be seen in Supporting information.

The synthesis of CDs was referred to the literature [18] with a slight modification. Firstly, 0.5 g of o-phenylenediamine was weighed and 300 μL of 0.1 mM FeCl3 aqueous solution was added. 50 mL of deionized water was added and sonicated for 10 min. The obtained solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-liner reactor and maintained in an oven at 100 ℃ for 9 h. After cooling to room temperature, an aqueous solution of carbon spots was obtained by vacuum filtration to remove impurities. The solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 3 d. And then it was freeze-dried to get CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the ability of the prepared CDs to detect water content in organic solvents, the following experiments were carried out. Firstly, DMF, MT and EA solutions with CDs content of 0.01 mg∙mL−1 were prepared respectively. Aqueous solutions of the same concentration of CDs were prepared simultaneously. Then different amounts of aqueous solutions of CDs were added into the organic solutions to make the test solutions with water content in 0 %–100 %, and then the fluorescence emission spectra of all test solutions were recorded.

Morphology and structure of the prepared CDs were studied first by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectroscopy. As showed in Fig. S1, they were well-dispersed and uniform with an average particle size of 2.75 nm. Fig. 1 a showed high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image. It clearly demonstrated the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, which was consistent with the (110) facet of graphitic structure and suggested monolayered sp2 graphitic carbon, indicating the CDs possessed a crystalline core [19,20]. Raman spectrum of the CDs was exhibited in Fig. 1b. Two obvious scattering peaks were observed at 1376 cm−1 and 1604 cm−1, respectively, which were assigned to D band and G band. Generally, the intensity ratio of D band and G band can be used to quantify thedefect densityin graphite.The calculated intensity ratio ID/IG = 2.53, implying highly disordered structure [21].

Chemical structure of CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak at 3422 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H, which proved the existence of amino and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. In addition, the stretching vibration of C–H causes strong peaks at ∼2968 cm−1 [22]. The characteristic peaks at 1619 cm−1 and 1505 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O [23]. The peak at 1411 cm−1 came from the stretching vibration of C-N, while the absorption bands located at 1221 cm−1 and 1067 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of C-O. Besides, the peaks at 1387 cm−1 and 876 cm−1 corresponded to C–H symmetric bending of –CH3 and aromatic C–H out-of-place bending vibration of benzene ring, respectively [24]. Above results conformed the prepared CDs possessed abundant surface functional groups.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to analyze the surface elemental composition and chemical states of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, XPS full spectrum of CDs showed the presence of high contents of C, N and O in CDs, which indicated that FeCl3 indeed catalysed oxidative polymerization. The C 1s fine spectrum (Fig. 3b) was deconvoluted into three components, among which the peak at the binding energy of 284.6 eV was attributed to C–C, the peak appeared at 288.3 eV was assigned to C=O/C=N and the peak at 283.7 eV resulted from graphite carbon, which indicated the CDs were highly graphitized. In the spectrum of N 1s region, besides the amino N at 401.4 eV, high contents of pyrrolic N and pyridine N were also observed at 400.2 eV and 398.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c). Appearance of the peaks of 533.6 eV and 531.8 eV in the O 1s high resolution spectrum proved the existence of C–O and C=O (Fig. 3d). These results were highly consistent with the results of infrared analysis.

The synthesized CDs are highly soluble in water and also soluble in common polar solvents, which indicates they possess abundant surface hydrophilic groups. The luminescent properties of the CDs in different organic solvents were investigated. First, the same amount of CDs was dissolved in the same volume of organic solvent and water, respectively and their fluorescence spectra were recorded. The normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in different organic solvents were showed in Fig. 4 a, in which the excitation wavelengths were λex(H2O) = 437 nm, λex(DMF) = 418 nm, λex(MT) = 429 nm, and λex(EA) = 424 nm, respectively. With the polarity of the solvent increasing (Polarity index of H2O, methanol, ethanol, and N, N-dimethylformamide is 10.2, 6.6, 6.5 and 4.3 [25], the emission peak of CDs gradually red-shifted. However, DMF is an exception. These results may be caused by the different surface states of CDs in different polar solvents. In addition, the CDs showed the same emission wavelength under different excitation wavelengths in all four solvents (Fig. S2), implying excitation-independent nature, which indicated surface of the CDs has been passivated by abundant groups [26] and the surface state distribution of CDs in different solvents was stable [27]. At the same time, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4b) also showed that the absorption peak of the CDs red-shifted with the solvent polarity, and the absorption peak redshifted with the increase of polarity. The different luminescence properties of CDs in these four solvents were due to solvent relaxation and dipole recombination of the solvent molecules around them promoting Stokes shift to higher values [28].

Additionally, the luminescence intensity of the CDs in four solvents presented the order: EA > DMF > MT > water, which was just opposite to redshift order except in DMF. It should be related to hydrogen bond formation between the solvent molecule and surface groups of the CDs (Fig. S3).

The fluorescence response of CDs to different water content in different solvents was investigated. The normalized fluorescence responses of CDs to the water contents in DMF were shown in Fig. 5 a. With the increase of the water content, the fluorescence emission peak redshifted. It was found that there were good linear relationships between fluorescence emission wavelength and the volume percentage of water in the ranges of 0 %–10 %, 10 %–40 % and 40 %–100 %, respectively (Fig. 5b), and the linear correlation coefficients (R2) were R1 2 = 0.9940, R2 2 = 0.9962 and R3 2 = 0.9927, respectively. Meanwhile, Fig. 5c, d also showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs vs the water content in DMF was linear in the ranges of 0 %–50 % (R1 2 = 0.9940) and 50 %–100 % (R2 2 = 0.9962) and the detection limit of H2O in DMF was down to 0.079 %. Meanwhile, Fig. 5c showed that with the water content increasing, the fluorescence emission peak gradually redshifted from 513 nm to 549 nm and the full width at the half-peak increased from 69 nm to 77 nm. This widening of the emission band may be caused by the longer non-radiative relaxation of CDs in highly polar solvents [29]. In addition, DMF solutions with different water contents under the irradiation of UV lamp in Fig. 5e also showed a clear color change from green to orange. Moreover, with the water content in the organic solvent increasing, the fluorescence of the CDs gradually darkened, which was consistent with the results of fluorescence emission spectra. It provided a possibility for visual monitoring of water content in organic solvent. The fluorescence responses of CDs in methanol and ethanol to different water contents were also investigated. As shown in Figs. S4 and S5, the fluorescence wavelengths and intensities also were linear with the water content in the two solvents and the calculated detection limits of H2O in MT and EA through the intensity-content plots were 0.074 % and 0.097 % respectively. In spite of some reports on the detection of water content in organic solvents, by comparison it was found that our developed method possessed comparative or even lower detection limit and wider linear range (Table 1 ). Especially, an applicability in almost full concentration range from 0 to 100 %(v/v) suggested its huge application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water response mechanism of luminescence of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this present paper, solvent-dependent emitting CDs were prepared and the as-synthesized CDs showed multi-color emission and excitation-independent luminescence features in different solvents (Fig. S1). This kind of excitation-independent photoluminescence is conductive to their chemical and biological sensing as well as bioimaging.

The excitation-independent emission was caused by complete passivation of CDs surface by amino (–NH2) and oxygen-containing groups, which made the CDs only exhibit inherent PL property of the CDs [26,33].

On the other hand, luminescence of the prepared CDs showed strong solvent polarity-dependent characteristics (Fig. 4, S1 and S2). Their maximum emission wavelength gradually redshifted and luminescence intensity decreased with polarity of the solvent enhancing (Fig. S6). It is believed to mainly due to vibrational relaxation. The relaxation or dipole recombination of surrounding solvent molecules impels the Stokes shift to a higher value simultaneously accompanied by a decrease in emission intensity [28,34]. And as the solvent polarity increases, the redshift becomes more pronounced based on Lippert-Mataga equation [35–37]. An exception is that DMF showed smaller redshift than EA (Fig. S6a), which might be because EA, as protic solvent, possessed a stronger hydrogen bond interaction with the surface groups in the CDs than aprotic DMF. Thus, it can be speculated that solvent relaxation caused by dipole and hydrogen bond interactions is main reason to cause the emission peak redshift of the CDs. Obviously, protic solvents are more effective in increasing the Stokes shift of carbon dots. For solvent-caused emission decay, the fluorescence intensity revealed the same order as the polarity increasing order (Figs. S4 and S6b), which indicated the polarity induced dipole recombination was a main reason resulting in fluorescence quenching.

In addition, as polarity of solvent enhanced the n → π* transition absorption band also redshifted to a higher longer wavelength besides fluorescence emission peak, which implied the decrease of the excited state energy level of the fluorophore (Fig. 4b). Obviously, strong interaction between the excited state of CDs and polar solvent molecule lowered the excited state level and further caused the redshift of the emission peak and absorption peak.

Based on above analysis, just because of stronger interaction between the polar CDs and water molecules, which had the highest polarity in the studied four solvents, presence of water component in the mixed solvent led to redshift of emission peak of the CDs and reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. As the water content in different organic solvents increased, the organic component in the mixed solution was gradually replaced by water molecules and polarity of the solution and hydrogen bond interaction with the surface polar groups in the CDs gradually enhanced. As a result, solvent relaxation and dipole recombination were improved and resulting vibrational relaxation was enhanced, which caused a solvent-induced color change and brightness reduction of the CDs, that is, so-called solvatochromic effect [38].

In this study, a simple and sensitive fluorescence sensor only based on CDs for the detection of water content in organic solvent was constructed by a one-step hydrothermal procedure. The prepared carbon dots showed different fluorescence in several different solvents, such as water, methanol, ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide, and with the increase of solvent polarity the fluorescence emission peak obviously red-shifted and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased. By studying the fluorescence response of the carbon dots in organic solvents with different water contents, it was found that carbon dots can be exploited for the sensitive detection of water content in these organic solvents. The developed fluorescence sensing method had a wider linear range and higher sensitivity than previous reports. Particularly, this method was applicable to determination of water with full concentration from 0 % to 100 %. This will be conducive to the rapid detection of water content in organic solvents in laboratory research work and industrial production.

Borong Ren: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Yinsheng Liu: Resources. Liping Peng: Software. Yingfei Hui: Supervision. Jiaxi Xu: Software. Wu Yang: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22364020), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23), the Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE and Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124932.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The light of carbon dots: from mechanism to applications
